title:
Module 2
Base Model Creation
subtitle: Learn How to Generate Your Base Model Realistically and Efficiently

blocks.content: Before you generate a single image, you need to decide who your AI model is. This is a business decision, not an aesthetic one. Your niche determines your audience, your content style, and ultimately your revenue potential.

items: Age range and look (18-25 is the standard sweet spot)
Ethnicity and features
Style and aesthetic (e.g., girl-next-door, alt, fitness, elegant)
Personality tone (shy, bold, playful, mysterious)
Name — must sound real, be memorable, easy to search

left.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
left.content: Examples of different AI persona styles/niches side by side

items: Pick a niche with proven demand — don't invent one
Research what's working on Fanvue and similar platforms
Your persona must feel like a real person, not a character
Avoid overly specific niches that limit content variety

right.content:
Niche: Cute, approachable college-age girl with a soft aesthetic.
Name: Emma, Lily, Sofia — simple, real, memorable.

Niche: 'Cyberpunk alien princess from the future.'
Name: XxDarkAngelxX — unnatural, forgettable.

blocks.content: Your base model is the core face and body that will remain consistent across all content. Getting this right is the single most important step. If the face changes between images, your entire page looks fake.

items: Choose your AI generation tool
Write a detailed base prompt describing your persona
Generate multiple variations
Select the strongest result as your 'seed' image
Save this image — it becomes your reference for everything

left.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
left.content: Screenshot of AI generation tool with base model prompt and output

right.content: What Makes a Good Base Model

items: Clear, well-lit face with visible features
Natural pose — not overly stylized or dramatic
Realistic skin texture and proportions
No artifacts, extra fingers, or distorted features
A face that can be recreated consistently

right.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
right.content: Good base model example vs bad base model example (artifacts, inconsistency)

blocks.title: Critical Rule
blocks.content: Your base model is your brand. Every image you generate from this point forward must look like the same person. If you cannot recreate the face reliably, choose a different seed image.

Consistency is what separates a believable AI influencer from an obvious fake. Your audience will scroll through your page. If the face changes between posts, trust is destroyed instantly. This lesson covers how to lock in your model's look.

items: Same face structure across all images — no exceptions
Same body type and proportions
Hair color and style should only change naturally (like a real person would)
Skin tone must remain constant
Eye color locked in from the start

left.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
left.content: Side-by-side grid showing consistent face across 4-6 different images/settings

items: Lighting and shadows (indoor vs outdoor)
Clothing and outfits
Background and location
Makeup style (subtle changes only)
Camera angle and distance

right.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
right.content: Example showing natural variation (lighting, outfit) while maintaining face consistency

blocks.title: Visual Consistency Check
blocks.content: Before posting any image, place it next to your last 3 posts. If the face does not look like the same person at a glance, do not post it. Regenerate until it matches.

You should never be scrambling for content. Before you post anything publicly, build a library of at least 30-50 images. This gives you a buffer and lets you plan your content calendar without pressure.

items: Set aside dedicated sessions for content generation
Generate 10-20 images per session
Vary outfits, settings, and poses across the batch
Sort images into categories: casual, lifestyle, glam, close-up
Keep a 'rejected' folder — you may salvage later

left.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
left.content: Screenshot of organized content library folder structure

items: Profile photos and headshots (3-5)
Casual lifestyle shots (10-15)
Glamour or dressed-up shots (5-10)
Close-up selfie-style images (5-10)
Story-ready casual content (10+)

right.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
right.content: Grid of example content library showing variety across categories

blocks.title: Minimum Before Launch
blocks.content: Do NOT launch any social media account until you have at least 30 quality images saved and sorted. Running out of content mid-warmup kills momentum and consistency.

summary_points: Choose your niche based on market demand, not personal preference
Your base model's face is your brand — consistency is everything
Generate a strong seed image and use it as reference for all future content
Lock in face, body type, skin tone, and eye color from day one
Lighting, outfits, and backgrounds can vary naturally
Build a library of 30-50 images before launching any account
